one_line_takeaway: A practical black-box defense that inserts an SLM-driven filtration layer into RAG and uses a new 'Freq-Density' word-concentration statistic to strip PoisonedRAG-style poisoned passages before generation, cutting attack success from ~80-94% to single digits while staying near clean-RAG accuracy on GPT models (though notably weaker with a LLaMA-3 generator).
key_finding: Across three QA benchmarks under black-box PoisonedRAG, both methods sharply reduce attack success while keeping accuracy near the clean (unpoisoned) baseline. With GPT-3.5, ML-FilterRAG reaches accuracies of 90.3% (HotpotQA), 85.1% (MS-MARCO), 81.1% (NQ) with ASRs of 9.0%, 6.0%, 2.0% and ATRs of 1.5%, 4.5%, 3.0% respectively; FilterRAG reaches 88.1%/82.0%/75.6% accuracy with ASRs 8.2%/9.0%/3.0% and ATRs 0.0%/6.5%/1.0%. For comparison the PoisonedRAG baseline drives ASR to roughly 82-94% and ATR near 1.0. ML-FilterRAG generally beats FilterRAG on accuracy because combining multiple features avoids the single-threshold misclassification of clean texts. Ablations show similarity threshold ~0.6 is the sweet spot (too-high 1.0 lets synonym-based evasion through; too-low blurs the clean/adversarial boundary), epsilon = 0.2 balances the ATR-vs-accuracy trade-off, and a larger SLM (LLaMA-3 > LLaMA-2) improves detection.
core_contribution: The paper proposes two black-box defenses against the PoisonedRAG knowledge-poisoning attack: FilterRAG (threshold-based) and ML-FilterRAG (machine-learning-based). Both insert an additional 'filtration' phase between a RAG system's retrieval and generation stages that removes adversarial passages from the retrieved context before they reach the generation LLM. The central novel idea is a new statistical property, Freq-Density, which measures the concentration of query-answer-related words within a retrieved text; the authors' hypothesis is that PoisonedRAG adversarial passages, because they must simultaneously rank highly for the target query and steer the LLM toward the attacker-desired answer, contain a dense overlap of query-related and answer-related words that clean passages lack. The authors also introduce a new evaluation metric, Adversarial Text Ratio (ATR), quantifying the fraction of adversarial texts surviving into the top-k. These are the paper's OWN contributions; other poisoning attacks (GARAG, PRCAP, Topic-FlipRAG) and other defenses (LLM-activation detection by Tan et al., ensemble/certified defense by Xiang et al., PoisonedRAG's own paraphrase/perplexity mitigations) are cited as prior work, not claimed here.
threat_model: Adopted directly from PoisonedRAG (Zou et al. 2024). An attacker pre-selects target questions Q and corresponding attacker-desired responses R, then injects n poisoned texts per target question directly into the RAG knowledge database D so that the compromised system returns r_i instead of the correct answer for q_i (canonical example: answering 'Syphilis' instead of 'AIDS' for a query about HIV). The paper considers both a black-box retriever setting (attacker cannot access retriever parameters or query it) and a white-box setting (attacker knows retriever parameters, e.g. a public retriever such as UAE-Large-V1, justified via Kerckhoffs's principle, using HotFlip to craft texts). Crucially, the DEFENDER (the paper's method) operates strictly in the black-box setting with respect to the generation LLM: it needs no access to model internals/activations, unlike Tan et al.'s prior defense.
limitations: The authors acknowledge the defense is validated ONLY against PoisonedRAG and is untested against other poisoning families (emotional-based, topic-flipping such as Topic-FlipRAG, low-level-perturbation GARAG), uses only simple ML classifiers, and adds a filtration component whose computational overhead is not rigorously analyzed. Beyond the authors' own list: (1) the evaluation uses an artificial balanced setup (top-s=4 with exactly equal adversarial/clean split, top-k=2) that the paper itself concedes does not reflect real retrieval where the adversarial proportion is unknown, likely flattering the results. (2) The headline 'performance gap of merely up to 0.2% vs original RAG' is cherry-picked from the strong GPT columns; with LLaMA-3 as the generation model the filters degrade badly (e.g. HotpotQA accuracy ~0.54 for both methods vs 0.665 for CleanRAG, and ATR/ASR remain high at ~0.33), a gap the abstract and conclusion understate. (3) ML-FilterRAG requires labeled adversarial training data per dataset (5 real + 5 GPT-4o-generated adversarial texts per question), a supervised requirement that may not generalize to unseen attack styles. (4) By construction the methods cannot exceed CleanRAG accuracy because some clean texts are always misclassified as adversarial.

datasets_used: Natural Questions (NQ) - 2,681,468 texts in knowledge DB; 100 target questions; XGBoost filter model
HotpotQA - 5,233,329 texts; 100 target questions; Random Forest filter model
MS-MARCO - 8,841,823 texts; 100 target questions; Random Forest filter model
PoisonedRAG-derived version (Zou et al. 2024): 5 adversarial texts injected per target question; +5 additional GPT-4o-generated adversarial texts for ML-FilterRAG training
quantitative_results: Black-box, GPT-3.5, ML-FilterRAG: accuracy 90.3% (HotpotQA) / 85.1% (MS-MARCO) / 81.1% (NQ); ASR 9.0% / 6.0% / 2.0%; ATR 1.5% / 4.5% / 3.0%
Black-box, GPT-3.5, FilterRAG (epsilon=0.2): accuracy 88.1% / 82.0% / 75.6%; ASR 8.2% / 9.0% / 3.0%; ATR 0.0% / 6.5% / 1.0%
PoisonedRAG baseline (black-box) ASR/ATR: HotpotQA ASR 94.0% ATR 1.000; MS-MARCO ASR 82.0% ATR 0.825; NQ ASR 88.0% ATR 0.980
CleanRAG accuracy (upper bound, GPT-3.5): HotpotQA 91.3%, MS-MARCO 85.9%, NQ 83.1%
LLaMA-3 generator weakness (black-box, HotpotQA): FilterRAG acc 0.536 / ML-FilterRAG 0.541 vs CleanRAG 0.665, with ATR ~0.015-0.38 and ASR ~0.33-0.38
epsilon ablation (MS-MARCO, GPT-4): epsilon=0.1 gives ATR 0.005/ASR 0.050/acc 62.4%; epsilon=0.2 best acc 84.0% (ATR 0.065, ASR 0.090); epsilon=0.5 ATR 0.765/ASR 0.540/acc 28.0%
Similarity-threshold ablation (MS-MARCO, GPT-4): optimum at 0.6 (FilterRAG acc 84.0%, ML-FilterRAG acc 84.9%); threshold 1.0 collapses (acc ~0.59-0.62, high ATR/ASR)
SLM effect (MS-MARCO, GPT-4): LLaMA-3 SLM beats LLaMA-2 (FilterRAG acc 0.786->0.840; ML-FilterRAG 0.791->0.849)
ML-FilterRAG classifier test accuracy: HotpotQA RF 0.982, NQ XGBoost 0.970, MS-MARCO RF 0.850
Config: top-s=4 (balanced adversarial/clean), top-k=2, m=10, 10 repeats; cosine similarity threshold default 0.6; temperature 0.1
baselines_compared: PoisonedRAG (Zou et al. 2024) - the attacked RAG system, lower bound
CleanRAG - traditional RAG with an unpoisoned knowledge database, upper bound

Table V qualitative comparison vs Tan et al. (LLM-activation detection), Xiang et al. (ensemble/certified defense), and Zou et al.'s own mitigations, distinguishing the proposed methods as black-box, non-ensemble, statistically-driven and retrieval-aware
